topic: Audio embeddings and aggregation over time
key_concepts: A fundamental difference between images and audio: audio is inherently sequential and unfolds over time. Processing a short audio clip produces a long sequence of frame-level representations.

The challenge of dimensionality: if a downstream classifier needs to predict one label per clip (e.g., classifying a genre or detecting a siren), the sequence of frames must be condensed into a single fixed-size vector.
Aggregation strategies: using pooling mechanisms along the time axis. Average pooling smooths out the background signal, while max pooling captures sudden acoustic events (like a glass breaking).

More advanced aggregation uses attention mechanisms, allowing the model to dynamically weight which time frames contain the most relevant semantic information before summing them into the final audio embedding.

definitions.definition: A dense vector representation that summarizes the acoustic and semantic content of an entire audio clip.
An aggregation operation that down-samples a representation, reducing its dimensionality while retaining its most important information.
A short, distinct segment of time within an audio signal.

examples: Imagine a 10-second recording of a quiet room suddenly interrupted by a dog barking at second 5. Average pooling might dilute the bark, but max pooling or attention will capture the bark's signature and use it to classify the clip correctly.

relations.builds_toward: ch14 (how aggregated audio vectors are concatenated with text/image vectors in multimodal setups)

exclusions: Do not dive into RNN/LSTM architectures; focus strictly on the pooling/aggregation of existing feature frames.
